heroOverview: Bagassosis is an occupational hypersensitivity pneumonitis caused by inhaling dust from bagasse — the dry fibrous residue of sugarcane stalks after juice extraction. The disease is mediated by a type III/IV immune hypersensitivity reaction to thermophilic actinomycetes, primarily Thermoactinomyces vulgaris and Thermoactinomyces sacchari, that colonise stored moist bagasse. Reported cases are concentrated in sugarcane processing and paper manufacturing industries across tropical and subtropical regions, including the Caribbean, South Asia, and Brazil. The acute form mimics influenza four to eight hours after heavy dust exposure; repeated or continuous exposure drives a chronic form with progressive interstitial fibrosis. Bagassosis is preventable through dust suppression engineering controls and personal respiratory protection, and early removal from exposure prevents most long-term lung damage.

keyStats.prevalence: Occupational disease; historically reported in 5–20% of workers with heavy bagasse exposure in uncontrolled environments before widespread engineering controls were adopted (Morgan & Seaton, 2004)
keyStats.demographics: Sugarcane mill workers, bagasse paper and board manufacturers, mushroom cultivators using bagasse compost; predominantly working-age adults in tropical and subtropical countries
keyStats.avgAge: Primarily affects working-age adults aged 20–60 in relevant industries; no sex predilection when occupational exposure is equal
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global burden unknown; significant case series have been reported from Louisiana (USA), Barbados, India, Brazil, and Australia; modern engineering controls have substantially reduced incidence in regulated workplaces

definition: Bagassosis (ICD-10: J67.1) is a form of extrinsic allergic alveolitis (hypersensitivity pneumonitis) caused by immunological sensitisation to organic antigens — predominantly the thermophilic actinomycetes Thermoactinomyces vulgaris (also formerly called Micropolyspora faeni) and Thermoactinomyces sacchari — present in mouldy or composted bagasse, the fibrous sugarcane residue. The disease falls within pulmonology and occupational medicine. Pathophysiologically, inhaled antigenic particles (1–5 µm in diameter) reach the alveoli, where they provoke immune complex deposition (type III hypersensitivity) and T-lymphocyte-mediated inflammation (type IV hypersensitivity), resulting in granulomatous alveolitis. In the acute phase, bilateral alveolar and interstitial infiltrates develop; with continued exposure, progressive interstitial fibrosis and restrictive ventilatory impairment can develop, mirroring the spectrum seen in farmer's lung, bird fancier's lung, and other hypersensitivity pneumonitides. Bagassosis is classified under the broader group of organic dust toxic syndrome and extrinsic allergic alveolitis (hypersensitivity pneumonitis) in occupational respiratory disease taxonomy. The key feature distinguishing bagassosis from infectious pneumonia is the temporal relationship to bagasse dust exposure and resolution with antigen avoidance. Precipitating antibodies (IgG precipitins) against Thermoactinomyces vulgaris and T. sacchari can be detected in serum and support the diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: Develops four to eight hours after heavy or peak bagasse dust exposure; presents with influenza-like illness, fever, cough, breathlessness, and malaise; resolves within days to weeks with antigen avoidance.
Results from lower-level but repeated exposure over weeks to months; produces a more gradual onset of cough, exertional dyspnoea, and weight loss without the dramatic acute attacks.
Caused by prolonged continuous or repeatedly recurrent exposure leading to irreversible interstitial fibrosis, progressive respiratory failure, and permanent restrictive lung function impairment.

primarySymptoms: Breathlessness (dyspnoea) on exertion, which in the acute form develops four to eight hours after a dust exposure episode and in chronic disease progresses insidiously over months.
A persistent dry or minimally productive cough, often worsening during peak bagasse processing seasons or high-dust workdays.
Fever, chills, and night sweats in the acute and subacute phases, reflecting the systemic inflammatory response to antigen inhalation.
Malaise and profound fatigue, typically appearing in the same four to eight hour post-exposure window as other acute symptoms.
Myalgia and headache accompanying the influenza-like systemic symptoms of acute attacks.
Chest tightness and pleuritic chest discomfort related to alveolar inflammation, particularly at the onset of acute episodes.
Fine bilateral inspiratory crackles audible on auscultation, particularly at lung bases, reflecting alveolar inflammation.
Weight loss and anorexia in subacute and chronic disease, reflecting the persistent systemic inflammatory burden.
Progressive exertional intolerance and reduced exercise capacity in chronic disease as fibrosis reduces lung compliance.
Cyanosis and finger clubbing in advanced chronic fibrotic disease, indicating severe hypoxaemia and prolonged alveolar damage.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurring influenza-like episodes (fever, cough, muscle aches) that begin several hours after heavy bagasse dust exposure and resolve on weekends or holidays away from work.
A pattern of feeling unwell during working shifts but recovering within two to three days of time off, which strongly suggests occupational hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
A dry persistent cough that started with a new job or at the beginning of a sugarcane crushing season.
Mild unexplained exertional breathlessness in a worker with known bagasse exposure, even without acute systemic symptoms.
emergencySigns: Severe breathlessness at rest or with minimal exertion indicating acute respiratory failure from a heavy exposure event.
Oxygen saturation below 92% on pulse oximetry during or after a dust exposure episode.
High fever (above 39°C) with severe respiratory distress in an acutely ill bagasse worker, which may also represent superimposed bacterial pneumonia.
Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)-pattern presentation after massive dust inhalation, requiring immediate hospital admission and ventilatory support.

causes.cause: Inhalation of Thermoactinomyces sacchari and T. vulgaris antigens in bagasse dust
causes.description: These thermophilic bacteria thrive in moist stored bagasse at temperatures of 45–60°C during spontaneous fermentation. Dried dust particles of 1–5 µm aerosolise during bagasse handling, shredding, or loading and penetrate to the alveoli, triggering immune sensitisation and subsequent hypersensitivity responses.

causes.cause: Repeated occupational inhalation of mouldy bagasse particles
causes.description: A single acute exposure can produce symptoms in previously sensitised individuals; repeated exposures over months to years drive progressive alveolar damage, granuloma formation, and eventually irreversible fibrosis, as seen in other forms of chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

causes.cause: Storage of moist bagasse enabling microbial proliferation
causes.description: Bagasse stored with high moisture content (above 20%) at ambient tropical temperatures creates the ideal incubation environment for thermophilic actinomycetes to multiply to the antigen loads sufficient to cause disease. Inadequate drying before storage is a key modifiable cause at the industrial level.

causes.cause: Immune sensitisation and type III/IV hypersensitivity
causes.description: After initial antigenic exposure, the immune system produces precipitating IgG antibodies against Thermoactinomyces antigens. Subsequent exposures trigger immune complex deposition in alveolar walls (type III) and a CD4+ T-lymphocyte granulomatous response (type IV), together causing alveolitis and, with chronicity, fibrosis.

causes.cause: Inadequate respiratory protection and dust control engineering
causes.description: Absence of effective exhaust ventilation, dust suppression systems, and appropriate respirators in bagasse handling facilities is the proximate cause of disease at the workplace level; case rates fall to near zero in facilities with effective engineering controls.

riskFactors.factor: Occupation in sugarcane milling or bagasse processing
riskFactors.description: Sugarcane mill workers, particularly those engaged in bagasse handling, loading, and storage, face the highest antigen exposure; historically reported prevalence of 5–20% sensitisation in exposed workers before engineering controls.

riskFactors.factor: Work in bagasse-based paper, board, or mushroom cultivation industries
riskFactors.description: Industries that use bagasse as a raw material outside of sugar production — paper mills, cardboard plants, mushroom farms using bagasse compost — expose workers to the same thermophilic actinomycetes and carry equivalent risk.

Workplaces without exhaust ventilation, dust suppression water sprays, or enclosed material handling systems have substantially higher airborne actinomycete counts and proportionally higher disease rates.

Failure to wear an FFP3/N100-class respirator during high-dust tasks (loading, shredding, cleaning) allows alveolar deposition of the fine particle fraction; standard surgical masks do not offer adequate protection.

Once immunologically sensitised, an individual may develop symptoms at lower antigen doses than the initial sensitising exposure; continued exposure in a sensitised worker dramatically accelerates the progression to chronic fibrotic disease.

Epidemiological studies of hypersensitivity pneumonitides, including farmer's lung, have noted lower rates of precipitin formation in smokers, attributed to tobacco-induced suppression of alveolar macrophage function; however, smoking independently causes severe lung disease and is not a safe strategy.

riskFactors.factor: High temperature and humidity in the work environment
riskFactors.description: Tropical and subtropical climates accelerate thermophilic actinomycete growth in stored bagasse; processing seasons in these regions coincide with periods of peak antigen load in the facility air.

affectedDemographics: Sugarcane mill and refinery workers involved in bagasse handling, storage, and transportation
Workers in bagasse paper, cardboard, and fibreboard manufacturing facilities
Mushroom cultivation workers using bagasse as growing medium compost
Agricultural workers in tropical and subtropical regions — principally the Caribbean, South Asia, South America, and Australia — where sugarcane cultivation is economically significant
Workers in older or less-regulated facilities lacking modern dust suppression systems
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of bagassosis rests on demonstrating the characteristic exposure history, compatible clinical and radiological features, and where possible serological or bronchoalveolar lavage evidence of immune sensitisation to bagasse antigens. The Schuyler criteria for hypersensitivity pneumonitis — modified for bagassosis — require: (1) symptoms consistent with hypersensitivity pneumonitis, (2) exposure to a recognised antigen in appropriate circumstances, (3) serum precipitins against the implicated antigen, (4) compatible chest radiograph or high-resolution CT, and (5) resolution on antigen avoidance. Lung function testing typically shows a restrictive pattern with reduced forced vital capacity (FVC), reduced total lung capacity (TLC), and reduced DLCO (diffusion capacity for carbon monoxide) in moderate to severe disease. High-resolution CT of the chest is more sensitive than plain radiography and shows ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodularity, and mosaic air trapping in acute and subacute disease, progressing to honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis in chronic fibrotic disease. Serum precipitating IgG antibodies against Thermoactinomyces vulgaris and T. sacchari are present in 40–80% of symptomatic patients and confirm immune sensitisation; however, they are also found in asymptomatic exposed workers, so they support rather than prove the diagnosis. Bronchoalveolar lavage typically shows lymphocytic alveolitis (CD4:CD8 ratio less than 1 in active disease). Surgical lung biopsy demonstrates non-caseating granulomata with lymphocytic alveolitis, distinguishing bagassosis from other interstitial lung diseases. The primary differential diagnoses are infectious pneumonia, farmer's lung (Thermoactinomyces vulgaris is a shared antigen), and other hypersensitivity pneumonitides.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of restrictive impairment and gas transfer reduction, used to stage disease severity and monitor progression or improvement following antigen removal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You breathe into a mouthpiece attached to a spirometer in a clinic; the test takes 20–30 minutes and involves several forced breathing manoeuvres. DLCO adds a single-breath carbon monoxide manoeuvre.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the characteristic ground-glass opacities, centrilobular nodularity, mosaic attenuation, and, in chronic disease, fibrotic changes including honeycombing that are not always visible on plain chest radiograph.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a CT scanner for a few minutes; no contrast injection is required for a standard HRCT. Radiation dose is low but not zero.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum precipitating antibodies (IgG precipitins) against Thermoactinomyces antigens
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms immune sensitisation to T. vulgaris and T. sacchari, supporting the diagnosis of bagassosis in the appropriate clinical context.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results are available from reference laboratories within a few days. A positive result does not confirm active disease alone, as asymptomatic exposed workers may also be seropositive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates lymphocytic alveolitis (typically CD4:CD8 ratio below 1) in active disease, supporting the diagnosis and helping to exclude infection and malignancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed during flexible bronchoscopy under conscious sedation; a saline wash of a lung segment is recovered and analysed. Mild sore throat and transient fever can follow the procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial assessment for bilateral interstitial or alveolar infiltrates; normal in early or mild disease but shows diffuse bilateral haziness in moderate acute attacks.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard two-view chest X-ray taking a few minutes; painless and involves a small radiation dose.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for confirming occupational hypersensitivity pneumonitis where diagnosis is uncertain; the worker is exposed to the suspected antigen under controlled conditions and monitored for a fall in FEV1 and systemic symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed only in specialist occupational lung disease units; involves controlled exposure to standardised antigen concentrations with serial lung function measurement over several hours to days. Not performed in established disease due to risk.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone of treatment for bagassosis is complete and permanent avoidance of further bagasse dust exposure, which halts immunological stimulation and allows resolution of acute alveolitis in most cases. In acute and subacute presentations with significant respiratory impairment, oral corticosteroids — typically prednisolone 40–60 mg/day — are prescribed for two to four weeks and then tapered over two to three months; this accelerates resolution of symptoms and improves lung function in the short term, though they do not alter the long-term fibrotic trajectory in chronic disease. Supplemental oxygen is provided for patients with resting hypoxaemia (SpO2 less than 92%). In the absence of contraindications, early removal from exposure combined with corticosteroid treatment typically produces near-complete recovery in acute and subacute cases. For established chronic fibrotic bagassosis that has progressed despite antigen avoidance, anti-fibrotic agents used for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis — nintedanib and pirfenidone — are under investigation in chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis; they are not yet standard practice for bagassosis specifically but may be considered in specialist centres managing progressive fibrotic disease. Pulmonary rehabilitation is important for all patients with chronic functional impairment. Secondary prevention — occupational health surveillance with periodic lung function testing and chest imaging for all bagasse-exposed workers — enables early detection before irreversible fibrosis develops. Workplace engineering controls (closed material handling, water spray dust suppression, exhaust ventilation) and appropriate respiratory protective equipment (FFP3/N100 respirators for high-dust tasks) are the most effective preventive interventions at the industrial level.

medicalTreatments.name: Complete antigen avoidance
medicalTreatments.description: Permanent removal from bagasse dust exposure is the primary and most effective treatment; in acute and subacute disease, avoidance alone leads to resolution in the majority of cases within weeks to months. Return to the same workplace without engineering controls is likely to accelerate fibrosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Near-complete resolution of acute and subacute disease in approximately 80–90% of patients who achieve sustained antigen avoidance before fibrosis is established.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: Prednisolone 40–60 mg/day for two to four weeks followed by a gradual taper over two to three months; prescribed for moderate to severe acute or subacute presentations to accelerate symptom resolution and lung function improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Accelerates clinical and radiological improvement within 2–4 weeks in acute disease; associated with approximately 30–40% faster recovery of FVC in controlled comparisons; does not prevent fibrosis if antigen exposure continues.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Long-term oxygen therapy for patients with resting hypoxaemia (PaO2 below 60 mmHg or SpO2 below 92%) to prevent secondary pulmonary hypertension and reduce mortality in chronic fibrotic disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Consistent with evidence in other fibrotic lung diseases showing reduced pulmonary hypertension progression and improved survival; specific bagassosis data are limited but consensus supports the intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: Structured exercise training, breathing techniques, and education programme for patients with chronic impairment; improves exercise tolerance, quality of life, and functional independence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses in chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis and ILD show approximately 30–50 metre improvement in six-minute walk distance and significant quality-of-life gains.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-fibrotic agents (nintedanib, pirfenidone)
medicalTreatments.description: Approved for progressive fibrotic interstitial lung disease; may be considered in specialist centres for patients with progressive chronic fibrotic bagassosis despite antigen avoidance, though specific evidence for bagassosis is extrapolated from the broader progressive fibrotic ILD literature.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In progressive fibrotic ILD trials, nintedanib reduces FVC decline by approximately 50% relative to placebo; evidence specific to bagassosis is limited to case reports and small case series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Workplace ergonomic and engineering modifications
alternativeTreatments.description: Water spray dust suppression, enclosed bagasse transport conveyors, exhaust ventilation, and pre-drying of bagasse before storage eliminate the antigen source; considered the most effective primary prevention intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: FFP3/N100 respirator use during high-dust tasks
alternativeTreatments.description: When complete exposure elimination is impossible, powered air-purifying respirators (PAPRs) with P3/HEPA filters or half-face FFP3 respirators reduce airborne exposure substantially; standard surgical or FFP1 masks are inadequate for actinomycete particle sizes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Breathing exercises and physiotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Physiotherapy-directed pursed-lip breathing, diaphragmatic breathing, and airway clearance techniques help manage chronic breathlessness and secretion clearance in patients with established lung damage.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist with experience in occupational and interstitial lung disease should be involved whenever bagassosis is suspected, to confirm the diagnosis, assess disease stage, and guide treatment decisions including corticosteroid use and fitness-for-work assessment. An occupational physician or occupational hygienist should conduct a workplace risk assessment to implement effective engineering controls and advise on safe redeployment. Patients developing progressive fibrotic disease should be referred to a specialist ILD multidisciplinary team.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a respiratory physician or pulmonologist with specific experience in hypersensitivity pneumonitis and occupational lung disease. Access to bronchoalveolar lavage, HRCT interpretation, and serum precipitin testing is important for accurate diagnosis. For workplace management, an occupational health physician or occupational hygienist with industrial hygiene capabilities should assess dust exposure levels and recommend engineering controls. Specialist ILD centres managing progressive fibrotic disease have access to multidisciplinary teams including radiologists, pathologists, and physiotherapists with relevant expertise.
preventionStrategies: Dry bagasse to moisture content below 15% before storage to prevent thermophilic actinomycete proliferation, the most effective primary prevention measure at the industrial level.
Install exhaust ventilation, enclosed conveying systems, and water-spray dust suppression in bagasse handling areas to reduce airborne particle counts below occupational exposure limits.
Provide and mandate use of FFP3/N100 or powered air-purifying respirators for all workers engaged in high-dust tasks such as bagasse loading, shredding, and cleaning.
Implement pre-employment and annual occupational health surveillance including spirometry and chest imaging for all workers with regular bagasse dust exposure.
Educate workers to recognise the characteristic post-shift symptom pattern of acute hypersensitivity pneumonitis and report it promptly before irreversible fibrosis develops.
Permanently redeploy workers who have developed sensitisation and bagassosis symptoms to low-exposure roles, as continued exposure in sensitised individuals accelerates fibrosis.
lifestyleModifications: Stop smoking completely; cigarette smoke independently damages alveoli and compounds the fibrotic changes of chronic bagassosis.
Maintain regular moderate aerobic exercise to preserve cardiopulmonary reserve and slow functional decline in patients with established lung disease.
Achieve and maintain a healthy body weight to reduce the additional ventilatory load of obesity on a lung with reduced compliance.
Ensure vaccination against influenza and Streptococcus pneumoniae to protect against respiratory infections that can cause acute deterioration in patients with impaired lung function.
Minimise exposure to any environmental organic dusts outside of work (mouldy compost, hay storage, bird droppings) that may contribute additional antigenic stimulation.

recommended: A balanced diet with adequate protein to support muscle maintenance and immune function during prolonged corticosteroid treatment
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, fortified plant milks, leafy greens) and vitamin D supplementation to offset osteoporosis risk during corticosteroid therapy
Anti-inflammatory food patterns including oily fish, vegetables, and fruits that may support lung health in chronic inflammatory disease
avoid: Excessive salt and refined carbohydrates during corticosteroid courses, which worsen fluid retention, hypertension, and glycaemic control
Alcohol in excess, which impairs respiratory muscle function and interacts with medications

exerciseGuidelines: Pulmonary rehabilitation with supervised aerobic exercise is recommended for all patients with moderate to severe bagassosis. Exercise intensity should be adjusted to maintain SpO2 above 92%; supplemental oxygen during exercise may be required in patients with significant hypoxaemia. Incremental walking programmes, cycling, and upper limb exercises are all appropriate. Strenuous exercise should be avoided during acute flares until systemic symptoms resolve. Regular exercise prevents muscle wasting, reduces breathlessness perception, and improves quality of life even when lung function cannot be fully recovered.
dailyManagement: Complete the prescribed corticosteroid taper as directed; do not stop abruptly without medical guidance.
Use peak flow or home pulse oximetry monitoring if prescribed, and report significant declines promptly.
Attend scheduled spirometry and HRCT follow-up appointments to monitor for disease progression or recovery.
Use respiratory protective equipment consistently during any unavoidable dust exposure if return to the same workplace is necessary.
Report any recurrence of post-shift fever, cough, or breathlessness immediately to the treating pulmonologist or occupational physician.
prognosis: The prognosis for bagassosis depends critically on the stage at which antigen avoidance is achieved. Acute and subacute disease diagnosed early and managed with prompt removal from exposure — with or without corticosteroids — carries an excellent prognosis, with most patients recovering full or near-full lung function within weeks to months. Patients who continue to be exposed despite symptoms and develop chronic fibrotic bagassosis face irreversible lung function impairment; the degree of fibrosis determines residual functional capacity and quality of life. Mortality from bagassosis itself is uncommon when diagnosis is early, but respiratory failure from progressive fibrosis is a recognised long-term outcome in patients diagnosed at an advanced stage. Avoidance of further exposure prevents new episodes in previously affected workers, though already established fibrosis does not regress.
recoveryTimeline: In acute bagassosis following a single heavy exposure, symptoms typically begin to improve within 24–48 hours of leaving the dusty environment. Full resolution of acute symptoms occurs in most cases within one to two weeks. With corticosteroid treatment, radiological improvement on HRCT begins within two to four weeks. Lung function recovery — measured by spirometry and DLCO — typically returns to near-baseline within two to three months in acute disease with sustained antigen avoidance. Subacute disease may take three to six months for functional recovery. Established chronic fibrotic disease does not resolve; management goals shift to slowing progression and maintaining exercise capacity.
complications: Progressive irreversible interstitial pulmonary fibrosis in workers who remain exposed despite symptoms, leading to permanent restrictive lung disease and reduced exercise capacity.
Secondary pulmonary hypertension developing in chronic fibrotic disease, worsening dyspnoea and exercise intolerance and requiring specific management.
Recurrent acute episodes ('acute-on-chronic') in sensitised individuals re-exposed to bagasse dust, each accelerating the fibrotic process.
Corticosteroid-related complications from prolonged treatment, including osteoporosis, glucose intolerance, cataracts, adrenal suppression, and susceptibility to infections.
Respiratory failure requiring long-term domiciliary oxygen therapy in severe chronic disease.
Occupational disability and unemployment with associated psychological burden in workers whose career in the sugarcane industry is terminated by the diagnosis.

sources.title: Morgan WKC, Seaton A. Occupational Lung Diseases, 3rd edition. WB Saunders, 2004
Lacasse Y et al. Clinical diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, 2003

confusedWithConditions.name: Farmer's lung
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Farmer's lung is hypersensitivity pneumonitis from mouldy hay (Thermoactinomyces vulgaris — a shared antigen), but occurs in agricultural rather than sugarcane processing occupations; the two share identical pathophysiology and treatment principles.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Occupational asthma from organic dust causes wheeze and airway reversibility on spirometry (obstructive pattern), whereas bagassosis causes an alveolitis with restrictive impairment and reduced DLCO; the two can coexist but require different treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Occupational asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Occupational asthma involves IgE-mediated bronchoconstriction with wheeze and variable airflow obstruction; bagassosis is IgG-precipitin-mediated alveolitis with a restrictive deficit, fever, and lymphocytic BAL.

relatedConditions.name: Farmer's lung
relatedConditions.relevance: The most analogous condition: same thermophilic actinomycete antigens, same pathophysiology, same treatment; differs only in the source material (hay vs bagasse) and occupational setting.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another occupational interstitial lung disease causing progressive restrictive impairment; shares the clinical and functional features of chronic fibrotic lung disease and the principle that avoidance of the causative dust is central to management.

relatedConditions.name: Silicosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A major occupational pneumoconiosis sharing restrictive lung function impairment and progressive fibrosis with chronic bagassosis; part of the broader differential diagnosis of occupational interstitial lung diseases.

relatedConditions.name: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another progressive occupational fibrotic lung disease caused by dust inhalation; differs mechanistically (non-immunological) but shares clinical features, management principles, and the critical role of exposure reduction.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: An occupational obstructive lung disease with similar exposure context in industrial settings; important to distinguish from bagassosis because the treatment principles and management of residual work capacity differ.

faqs.question: What is bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Bagassosis is an occupational lung disease — a type of hypersensitivity pneumonitis — caused by inhaling dust from mouldy bagasse, the fibrous residue of sugarcane after juice extraction. The immune reaction to fungal bacteria in the dust inflames the lung's air sacs.

faqs.question: What causes bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Bagassosis is caused by inhaling antigens from thermophilic bacteria — mainly Thermoactinomyces sacchari and Thermoactinomyces vulgaris — that grow in stored moist bagasse. The immune system reacts abnormally to these antigens, causing lung inflammation.

faqs.question: Who is at risk of bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Workers in sugarcane mills, bagasse paper and board factories, and mushroom farms using bagasse compost face the highest risk. The disease is most common in tropical and subtropical countries where sugarcane processing occurs, including the Caribbean, India, Brazil, and Australia.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Acute bagassosis causes flu-like symptoms — fever, cough, breathlessness, muscle aches — starting 4–8 hours after dust exposure. Chronic bagassosis causes progressive breathlessness, persistent cough, and weight loss from irreversible lung fibrosis.

faqs.question: How is bagassosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines an occupational exposure history, typical symptoms with a post-shift pattern, HRCT chest scan showing ground-glass opacities, lung function tests showing restriction, and blood tests for precipitating antibodies to Thermoactinomyces antigens.

faqs.question: How is bagassosis treated?
faqs.answer: The primary treatment is permanent removal from bagasse dust exposure. For moderate to severe acute disease, oral prednisolone is prescribed for 2–4 weeks and tapered over 3 months. Supplemental oxygen and pulmonary rehabilitation help manage chronic functional impairment.

faqs.question: Can bagassosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Acute and subacute bagassosis resolves in most patients after removing the dust exposure, with near-full lung function recovery in 2–3 months. Chronic fibrotic bagassosis cannot be reversed once fibrosis is established; management aims to slow progression and preserve function.

faqs.question: Is bagassosis the same as farmer's lung?
faqs.answer: They are very closely related. Both are forms of hypersensitivity pneumonitis caused by the same thermophilic bacteria (including Thermoactinomyces vulgaris), with identical pathophysiology and treatment. The difference is the source: mouldy hay for farmer's lung, mouldy bagasse for bagassosis.

faqs.question: How can bagassosis be prevented at work?
faqs.answer: Prevention involves drying bagasse to below 15% moisture before storage, installing exhaust ventilation and dust suppression systems, providing FFP3/N100 respirators for high-dust tasks, and conducting regular lung function surveillance for all exposed workers.

faqs.question: What does bagasse mean?
faqs.answer: Bagasse is the dry fibrous material left over from sugarcane stalks after the juice has been squeezed out during sugar production. It is also used industrially as fuel, in paper manufacturing, and as compost for mushroom cultivation — all contexts associated with bagassosis exposure.

faqs.question: Does smoking cause bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Smoking does not cause bagassosis. In fact, paradoxically, smokers show lower rates of immune sensitisation (precipitin formation) to bagasse antigens — possibly due to tobacco-suppressed alveolar immunity. However, smoking causes severe independent lung disease and worsens the long-term outlook.

faqs.question: What does a chest CT show in bagassosis?
faqs.answer: HRCT shows bilateral ground-glass opacities and centrilobular nodularity in acute disease, often with mosaic air trapping on expiratory images. In chronic fibrotic disease, honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis develop, similar to other fibrotic interstitial lung diseases.

faqs.question: Is bagassosis the same as hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bagassosis is one specific form of hypersensitivity pneumonitis — the type caused by bagasse dust. Other forms include farmer's lung (hay), bird fancier's lung (bird droppings), and hot tub lung (Mycobacterium avium). The disease mechanism and treatment approach are the same.

faqs.question: What lung function tests are used for bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Spirometry shows a restrictive pattern (reduced FVC with preserved or increased FEV1/FVC ratio). Diffusion capacity (DLCO) is reduced, reflecting impaired gas transfer across inflamed or fibrotic alveolar walls. These tests are used at diagnosis and to monitor progress.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from acute bagassosis take?
faqs.answer: With removal from dust exposure, acute bagassosis symptoms begin improving within 24–48 hours. Most patients feel well within 1–2 weeks. Lung function and HRCT improvements take 2–3 months of antigen avoidance, longer in more severe cases.

faqs.question: Can someone with bagassosis return to work?
faqs.answer: A worker who has recovered from acute bagassosis should not return to the same dust-exposure environment without effective engineering controls in place. If exposure cannot be adequately controlled, permanent redeployment to a dust-free role is the safest option, advised by an occupational physician.

faqs.question: What steroids are used for bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Oral prednisolone at 40–60 mg/day is the standard treatment for moderate to severe acute or subacute bagassosis, tapered over 2–3 months. Steroids accelerate symptom and radiological recovery but do not reverse established fibrosis or eliminate the need for antigen avoidance.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of bagassosis?
faqs.answer: If caught early and exposure stopped, long-term effects are usually minimal. If exposure continues, progressive interstitial fibrosis causes permanent restrictive lung disease, reduced exercise capacity, and in severe cases respiratory failure requiring long-term oxygen. Recurrent acute attacks accelerate this process.

faqs.question: Is bagassosis common?
faqs.answer: Bagassosis is an uncommon occupational disease, concentrated in sugarcane processing regions. Before modern dust controls, sensitisation rates of 5–20% were reported in exposed workers. It is now rare in regulated facilities with effective dust suppression systems in place.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute and chronic bagassosis?
faqs.answer: Acute bagassosis follows a single heavy dust exposure with flu-like symptoms starting 4–8 hours later, resolving in days to weeks. Chronic bagassosis develops from prolonged or repeated exposure and causes irreversible interstitial fibrosis with permanent breathlessness and restrictive lung function loss.

metaDescription: Bagassosis is an occupational lung disease from sugarcane dust causing hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment options.
